So the code would look like:

class MyStack {
public:
    queue<int> q;

    MyStack() {
        
    }
    
    void push(int x) {
        int size = q.size();
        q.push(x);
        for (int i = 0; i < size; ++i) {
            q.push(q.front());
            q.pop();
        }
    }
    
    int pop() {
        int val = q.front();
        q.pop();
        return val;
    }
    
    int top() {
        return q.front();
    }
    
    bool empty() {
        return q.empty();
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
class MyStack {
public:
    std::queue<int> q;

    MyStack() {
        
    }
    
    void push(int x) {
        int size = q.size();
        q.push(x);
        for (int i = 0; i < size; ++i) {
            q.push(q.front());
            q.pop();
        }
    }
    
    int pop() {
        int val = q.front();
        q.pop();
        return val;
    }
    
    int top() {
        return q.front();
    }
    
    bool empty() {
        return q.empty();
    }
};
```
efficiency: MEDIUM
time complexity: O(N) for push, O(1) for others
space complexity: O(N)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Uses a single queue to simulate stack behavior. Push operation rotates elements to maintain LIFO order, achieving O(1) pop/top with O(N) push. Efficient for limited operations but suboptimal for frequent pushes.
